lines: I met a traveller from an antique land,
Who said—"Two vast and trunkless legs of stone
Stand in the desert. . . . Near them, on the sand,
Half sunk a shattered visage lies, whose frown,
And wrinkled lip, and sneer of cold command,
Tell that its sculptor well those passions read
Which yet survive, stamped on these lifeless things,
The hand that mocked them, and the heart that fed;
And on the pedestal, these words appear:
My name is Ozymandias, King of Kings;
Look on my Works, ye Mighty, and despair!
Nothing beside remains. Round the decay
Of that colossal Wreck, boundless and bare
The lone and level sands stretch far away."

deviations.note: The fourth and fifth feet fall as a pyrrhic and a spondee, dropping the line's weight onto 'antique land'.
A spondee slows the opening of the traveller's report, piling stress on the ruin's colossal scale.
An initial trochaic inversion plants the stressed verb 'Stand', so the legs rise on the line's first beat.
The opening spondee drops two heavy stresses, enacting the visage's collapse into the sand.
An initial trochee throws the stress onto 'Tell', the verb governing the whole boast-bearing sentence.
A trochaic inversion after the caesura stresses 'stamped', the act that fixed the passions onto stone.
The imperative 'Look' takes an inverted first foot, commanding the reader before the boast names its object.
'Nothing' opens with a trochaic inversion at the poem's turn, the stressed negation sweeping the boast away.
After the full stop, a second inversion reorients the poem outward to the surrounding waste.
'boundless' falls as a trochee, its stress on 'bound' clashing with 'Wreck' across the caesura to spread the emptiness.
The closing spondee clusters stresses to stretch the final line, miming sands that reach past sight.

elisionQuestions.prompt: How many syllables does 'traveller' take here—the full three, or two, elided to 'trav'ler' to hold the iambic line?
How many syllables does 'Ozymandias' take here—the elided four that keep the pentameter, or the full five of ordinary speech?

Two vast and trunkless legs of stone

sentences.paraphrase: Two huge stone legs, their torso long gone, stand upright in the desert.

anchor.exact: Near them, on the sand, Half sunk a shattered visage lies, whose frown, And wrinkled lip, and sneer of cold command, Tell that its sculptor well those passions read Which yet survive, stamped on these lifeless things, The hand that mocked them, and the heart that fed

sentences.paraphrase: Near the legs a half-buried, shattered stone face lies, its carved frown, lip, and sneer showing that the sculptor well understood the king's passions, which outlast both the hand that copied them and the heart that fed them.

gnarly.label: Main clause: a shattered visage lies (half sunk, near the legs, on the sand)

Compound subject: whose frown, And wrinkled lip, and sneer of cold command

Appositive naming the lifeless things: The hand that mocked them, and the heart that fed

And on the pedestal, these words appear

senses: ancient, of the distant past—here an ancient land, Egypt
without a trunk—the statue's torso is gone, leaving only the legs
the face, or the expression the face wears
a scornful, contemptuous curl of the lip
the base or plinth on which a statue or column stands
the process or state of falling into ruin; here, the crumbling remains themselves
imitated or copied—as an artist mocks a model, reproducing its features in stone
took in words from a page
discerned and interpreted the king's passions, reading a person's character as if it were text
to outlive, to outlast (transitive)—the passions outlast the hand and the heart that made them
of or like a colossus—a giant statue; the word's root recalls exactly such a monument

palette.name: irony
palette.definition: A gap between what is said or expected and what is actually the case—here the tyrant's claim to permanence set against the ruin that surrounds it.

canonicalExample.text: Water, water, every where, / Nor any drop to drink
canonicalExample.source: Coleridge, 'The Rime of the Ancient Mariner'

palette.name: alliteration
palette.definition: The repetition of the same initial consonant sound in nearby words.

palette.name: synecdoche
palette.definition: A figure in which a part stands for the whole (or the whole for a part).

canonicalExample.text: Was this the face that launch'd a thousand ships

palette.name: anastrophe
palette.definition: The inversion of ordinary word order—object before verb, adverbial before subject—for emphasis or metre.

canonicalExample.text: Of Man's first disobedience ... Sing, Heavenly Muse

palette.name: apostrophe
palette.definition: A direct address to an absent or imaginary person, a thing, or an abstraction.

canonicalExample.text: Death, be not proud, though some have called thee / Mighty and dreadful

palette.name: caesura
palette.definition: A strong pause within a line of verse, usually marked by punctuation.

canonicalExample.text: To err is human; to forgive, divine.

palette.name: enjambment
palette.definition: The running-over of a sentence or phrase from one line of verse into the next without a terminal pause.

canonicalExample.text: Dust as we are, the immortal spirit grows / Like harmony in music

anchor.exact: Look on my Works, ye Mighty, and despair

instances.functionNote: The tyrant's boast of unrivalled power now reads as its opposite—nothing of his works survives to be envied, only ruin.
The inscription turns from statement to direct address, commanding the powerful who might one day read it.
The sculptor's hand stands for the whole artist and his shaping craft.
The king's heart stands for the whole man and the ruling passions he nourished.

anchor.exact: its sculptor well those passions read

instances.functionNote: Object before verb—'read those passions well'—throws weight onto 'passions' and slows the clause to a reader's pace.
The hard paired c's stiffen the phrase, hardening the tyrant's authority into stone.
The paired b's bind the two words, and the open vowels stretch the emptiness they name.
The repeated l's flatten the line's music to match the flatness of the sands.
The full stop mid-line halts the poem at its turn, the silence enacting the void that 'nothing... remains'.
The line break holds the legs suspended before 'Stand' completes them, miming the eye's travel over the wreck.

broadDevices.speaker: The living voice is an unnamed first-person narrator ('I met a traveller'), who claims almost nothing of his own and merely relays what he was told; the traveller is the inner speaker who describes the statue; and Ozymandias himself speaks, long dead, only through the inscription the traveller quotes.
broadDevices.addressee: The frame poem addresses no one in particular—the reader, overhearing a traveller's tale. Its only explicit address is buried in the inscription, where Ozymandias apostrophises 'ye Mighty', the rival rulers he commands to look on his works and despair. That embedded, now-hollow command is the poem's rhetorical core.
broadDevices.pointOfView: Nested, receding narration: the poet reports a traveller, who reports the statue, which quotes the dead king. Each frame sets Ozymandias's boast further off, so his first-person 'I' reaches us third-hand, already ironised by the ruin the traveller has seen—the tyrant's voice survives only as mediated, diminished hearsay.

allusions.source: Ozymandias is the Greek name for the Egyptian pharaoh Ramesses II (reigned in the 13th century BCE), whose colossal mortuary statue at the Ramesseum in Thebes carried his throne-name, User-maat-re, Graecised by ancient writers as Ozymandias.
allusions.imports: Naming the pharaoh by his half-forgotten Greek title measures how far even the greatest king has slipped from the tongues that once feared him—the man endures only as a garbled foreign word.

anchor.exact: Look on my Works, ye Mighty, and despair

allusions.source: The inscription reworks the one Diodorus Siculus (1st century BCE) recorded on the Ramesseum colossus: 'King of Kings am I, Osymandias. If anyone would know how great I am and where I lie, let him surpass one of my works.' Shelley likely met it through Diodorus's history.
allusions.imports: Shelley keeps the tyrant's challenge but strips its triumph—where Diodorus's king dares rivals to outdo his works, Shelley's dares them to 'despair', and the surrounding desert has already answered him.

allusions.source: 'King of Kings' was the title of the Persian and Assyrian emperors, and in the King James Bible it names God and Christ (1 Timothy 6:15; Revelation 19:16).
allusions.imports: The most exalted title of the ancient world, claimed here by a king now nameless dust—and, to a Christian reader, a blasphemous usurping of a name reserved for God, which deepens the fall.

argument.bigQuestion: Can power, art, or empire secure a name against time?
argument.claim: Time defeats every claim to permanence—the tyrant's empire and boast have vanished into 'lone and level sands', and what little endures, the shattered art that caught his arrogance, preserves only that arrogance to be judged, not the power he trusted.
